summary: Gruber dissects a New York Times report that Tim Cook personally killed an Apple TV+ show based on Gawker, arguing that the article fails to explain the crucial 'why' behind the decision. He notes the show was a roman à clef, not a documentary, so Cook's personal animosity toward Gawker alone doesn't explain the cancellation — the show's tone and portrayal matter enormously. Gruber then pivots to what he considers the buried, far more important story: Apple's explicit corporate rule against portraying China negatively in any Apple TV+ content. He argues this China policy is genuinely scandalous, effectively removing major geopolitical realities from artistic treatment, and notes that Hollywood broadly shares this complicity due to Chinese market dependence.
key_points: The NYT story about Cook killing the Gawker show lacks the essential explanation of why — a roman à clef about Gawker could have been unflattering to Gawker, making Cook's motives ambiguous
Apple's explicit rule forbidding negative portrayals of China in Apple TV+ content is the real scandal buried in the story, effectively censoring geopolitical reality for commercial reasons
An equivalent Cold War-era rule about Russia would have killed the entire James Bond franchise and most spy films
Hollywood's broad dependence on the Chinese box office means Apple and Disney aren't outliers — almost no major studio would satirize China the way Chaplin satirized Hitler
Apple's reported rule against showing phones being damaged on screen reveals an absurdly overprotective brand-image mindset
money_quotes: My point here isn't to dispute the basic reporting that Cook nixed the show, but to observe that what Smith reported is really just the setup, not the complete story.
Are we supposed to be surprised, in the least, that it turns out Tim Cook runs Apple and has the final call over everything the company does?
This isn't gossipy — or the least bit surprising — but unlike the Gawker show getting nixed, this 'don't offend China' rule ought to be genuinely scandalous.
To take China off the table is to take much of what's going on geopolitically in the world today off the table.
Just because it's so transparently obvious why Apple would forbid any negative portrayals of China doesn't make it any less outrageous.

China has effectively foot-stomped and bribed its way into not being shown in movies for what it clearly is: a powerful, cruel, brutal Communist regime led by a thin-skinned dictator who does in fact quite resemble Winnie the Pooh.
A 'no showing phones getting damaged' rule, if true, makes Apple look as much in need of a pair of big boy pants as the leaders of China.
key_insight: The real story isn't that Tim Cook killed a Gawker show — it's that Apple explicitly forbids negative portrayals of China, a policy that is genuinely scandalous even if commercially understandable, and one shared across nearly all of Hollywood.
